narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 28, lower-middle family income background, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 2—meaning you placed in the lower end of your class compared to peers, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 51% chance you'll fail the bar exam, tipping the scale just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or hard work, but a data-driven assessment drawing from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of thousands of similar applicants. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive influence, pushing the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass because it's solidly above the typical 3.21 average for students in the dataset. Next in influence was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which had a notable negative pull, signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure since it falls well below the average 5.5-ish decile and aligns with profiles of students who struggled later on. Your lower-middle family income also helped your case somewhat, nudging the prediction toward passing as it matches a bracket that, on average, correlates with resilience in bar outcomes compared to extremes. Overall, these top three elements—your stronger-than-average undergrad grades counterbalanced by weaker class rankings and supported by your income level—were the key drivers outweighing other details like your LSAT or full-time status, which is common for 93% of applicants. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows promise in GPA and background but lags in school deciles, placing you in a borderline group where the model leans cautious. This isn't set in stone; many in similar situations, including Hispanic men from modest incomes, have boosted their paths forward. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to push toward the 37 average, seeking ways to improve class rankings through grad-level courses or targeted tutoring, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your grit. You might also explore bridge programs or community college pathways that have helped others climb deciles and pass the bar at higher rates. Keep pursuing this—your above-average GPA shows you have the foundation to turn this around.
